title: Human Capital Investment after the Storm
abstract: How does household exposure to a natural disaster affect higher education investments? Using variation in flooding from Hurricane Harvey (2017), we find that college-aged adults from flooded blocks in Houston are 7% less likely than counterparts to have student loans after Harvey, with larger effects in areas with more potential first-generation students. We find a similar relative decline in enrollment at more exposed Texas universities and colleges and a shift toward majors with higher expected earnings. Our results highlight a decrease in the quantity but an increase in the intensity of investments in human capital after the storm.

author.Emily A Gallagher*: Leeds School of Business,University of ColoradoBoulder,USA
author.Stephen B Billings: Leeds School of Business,University of ColoradoBoulder,USA
author.Lowell R Ricketts: Institute for Economic Equity,Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis,USA

article.1. Background: Hurricane Harvey:
JEL D12 - Consumer Economics: Empirical Analysis G51 - Household Saving, Borrowing, Debt, and Wealth I22 - Educational Finance; Financial Aid Q54 - Climate; Natural Disasters; Global Warming R00 - General Issue Section: Article Editor: Tarun Ramadorai Tarun Ramadorai Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

Higher education investment decisions have important consequences for lifetime earnings. A growing literature shows that economic conditions present at the time (e.g., job opportunities, tuition rates) can sway these investment decisions on both the extensive and intensive margins (Charles, Hurst, and Notowidigdo 2018;Blom, Cadena, and Keys 2021;Chakrabarti et al. 2020). As extreme weather events grow more frequent and severe due to climate change (Kossin 2018;NOAA 2020), natural disasters represent an increasingly important form of economic shock. It stands to reason that these events may affect individual finances and expectations in ways that differ from the settings explored in existing research on human capital formation, thus making the predicted effects of disastersa prioriunclear. To our knowledge, this paper represents the first empirical investigation of how a major U.S. natural disaster affected higher education investments.1

While a broad literature has explored human capital responses to recessions and housing cycles (Lovenheim 2011;Lovenheim and Reynolds 2013;Amromin, Eberly, and Mondragon 2017;Charles, Hurst, and Notowidigdo 2018;Blom, Cadena, and Keys 2021), natural disasters offer a rare setting in which substantial property wealth is lost but tuition and labor market trajectories remain largely unchanged. In addition, while spending typically falls during recessions, disasters destroy collateral, while increasing living expenses. Hence, liquidity constraints may be more likely to bind after disasters. Disasters may also update expectations as prospective students learn about uncertain and infrequently observed events that may threaten their future wealth. Whether and how these events shape human capital investments for different population subgroups is an empirical question.

We address this question using data on investments in higher education, including the use of student debt financing, enrollment, completion, and the fields of study pursued. Our setting is Hurricane Harvey, which submerged over a quarter of the Houston, Texas, metropolitan area in August of 2017. One-third of Harvey’s total precipitation and $67 billion of direct economic damages have been attributed to climate change (Frame et al. 2020).

Our first empirical test relies on the Federal Reserve Bank of New York/Equifax Consumer Credit Panel (CCP) from Q2 2015 to Q2 2021. From these data, we study the student loan outcomes of over 129,000 individuals, of which 9,000 were college-age with permanent addresses in the Houston metropolitan area just before the arrival of Harvey (Q2 2017). We join this data set to measures of flood intensity and floodplain coverage, gathered from FEMA at the Census block level. We employ a treatment intensity difference-in-differences (DiD) method, which compares changes in the student loan outcomes of individuals from heavily flooded Census blocks with those of individuals from the parts of Houston that did not flood. Our identification method relies on the assumption that the nonflooded parts of Houston serve as an informative counterfactual about the path of student debt in the flooded parts of Houston in the absence of Hurricane Harvey. To validate this assumption, we show that student debt in flooded and nonflooded blocks trended similarly in the lead-up to Harvey. We also document that much of the flooding in Houston was quasi-random; only about 7% of the variation in flooding across blocks can be explained by pre-determined socioeconomic and geospatial attributes.

We find substantial declines in the student loan borrowing of college-age individuals in the aftermath of Harvey. On average, over the 16 quarters post-Harvey, “college-aged” adults (ages 18 to 23) from heavily flooded areas are around 3 percentage points (7% of the mean) less likely to have student loans than are counterparts from parts of Houston that did not flood. There is also a negative intensive margin effect for college-aged borrowers – those with preexisting student debt grow their balances by about 5% less when they come from flooded neighborhoods. By comparison, flooding has a much more limited impact on the student loan use of older age groups.

Dissecting along other dimensions, we find flooding has a more negative effect on the student loan use of college-aged individuals without a history of prior student loans and those from areas with owner-occupied housing but lower incomes and fewer college graduates. As we will later discuss, one possible interpretation is that flooding affects the investments of prospective first-generation undergraduates – i.e., those most likely to be on the margin of college due to financial constraints (Chakrabarti et al. 2020;Denning, Marx, and Turner 2019). The relative decline in the use of student loans among college-aged adults does not reverse the next school year. Instead, potential undergraduates at the time of Harvey are still “missing loans,” relative to counterparts from nonflooded areas, 4 years later.2

Our second empirical test examines enrollment and completion outcomes. Since these outcomes are not available at the individual level with the geographic granularity that would be needed to assign flooding exposure, we use school-level administrative data from the Texas Higher Education Coordinating Board (THECB). Once again, we apply a DiD design, this time comparing Texas public universities and colleges with more versus fewer students originating from high schools in the 40 Texas counties that severely flooded during Hurricane Harvey.

The disaster appears to have negatively affected higher education attainment. After Harvey, schools with an above-median enrollment exposure to the disaster grow their enrollment by 4.8% less relative to other schools. Consistent with the student debt results, the youngest (college-aged) cohort incurs the most negative enrollment effect. The graduation rate, an indicator of completion, is also 1.8 percentage points lower at more treated schools after Harvey. We continue to find statistically and economically significant effects when we exclude schools located in disaster-affected counties, implying that students are not merely shifting their enrollment to schools outside of the physical range of the disaster. It follows that the relative decline in the use of student debt observed in credit data after Harvey may be explained, at least in part, by a relative decline in enrollment at more exposed schools.

Lastly, we use the THECB’s breakdown of enrollment by major within each school and semester to test for changes in the fields of study of enrolled students. At more treated schools, low earnings majors, specifically those with poor longer-term (i.e., 10-year) earnings prospects, lose students, while high-earnings majors gain students in these same schools. Specifically, within the more treated colleges, we find less enrollment in majors like communications tech support, liberal arts, and languages and more enrollment in biological sciences, business, and engineering. The latter gain is not enough, however, to offset the former effect in aggregate. These results may stem either from changes in the majors of previously enrolled students or from changes in the composition of students who enroll. We cannot distinguish between these two explanations with school-level data. Nevertheless, these results highlight a shift toward course-taking that is often considered more challenging and generates higher long-term earnings, possibly at the expense of studies that may be considered greater in consumption, rather than investment, value.

We evaluate several plausible mechanisms. First, liquidity constraints, if binding, would imply greater tuition price sensitivity after Harvey. Consistent with this prediction, we find that more treated community colleges lose a smaller share of students than more treated universities, particularly when the university is in the right tail of tuition costs per credit hour. Second, to identify wealth effects, we draw on a large literature (e.g.,Bulman et al. 2021) documenting that, relative to poorer households, wealthier households devote more of their education spending to courses that are greater in consumption (rather than investment) value. It follows that, as households lose wealth to flooding, we should expect more of their spending on higher education to be aimed at investment. This prediction is consistent with our finding of an intensive margin adjustment in enrollment toward majors that lead to higher earnings. Finally, we apply the conceptual model ofCharles, Hurst, and Notowidigdo (2018)to our data. They model equilibrium college attendance for individuals as a function of their ability, with bachelor’s degree students (at universities) being the highest ability. Their model predicts that community college students should dissenroll at higher rates than university students when labor market opportunity costs rise. Instead, we find that the negative treatment effect on community college enrollment is milder relative to universities, which is inconsistent with a labor market opportunity cost mechanism.

In terms of broader implications, our results suggest that natural disasters can have important effects on the life chances of young people over and above their direct impacts on parental wealth. Less aggregate college attainment implies distributional consequences since the marginal college student tends to both graduate and achieve large gains in earnings (Denning, Marx, and Turner 2019). And, since these effects persist for at least 4 years, Harvey may have led to a permanent “educational scarring” of certain subsets of cohorts, in keeping with the often enduring nature of education decisions (Charles, Hurst, and Notowidigdo 2018). While our data and setting are too limited to test for long-term effects on the income distribution, such effects would be in accord with a longitudinal study (Howell and Elliott 2018) documenting an expansion of wealth inequality in counties affected by disasters. Simply put, education may be one channel through which disasters propagate inequality. On the other hand, even if our results portend a widening earnings gap between college and noncollege graduates in disaster-affected areas, aggregate productivity may benefit from more graduates trained in fields that command higher earnings. Thus, the net long-term welfare impact of disasters remains to be determined.

To the extent that the distributional consequences of disasters warrant intervention, there are several ways that policy changes could help mitigate the effects documented in this paper. Foremost, no Federal programs are aimed at keeping disaster-affected students enrolled and financial aid formulas fail to account for recent financial shocks. Policy makers may also consider adjusting current disaster assistance rules to prioritize the recoveries of those households that are least able to absorb a financial shock. Namely, lower-income households are least likely to receive both Federal disaster assistance and loans (Begley et al. 2021;Billings, Gallagher, and Ricketts 2022) and, as our results would suggest, most likely to be on the margin of going to college due to liquidity constraints.
Hurricane Harvey was a Category 4 hurricane that made landfall on August 25, 2017, and dumped 27 trillion gallons of rain on Houston, Texas. This single storm was the largest amount of rainwater ever recorded in the continental United States. The storm submerged over a quarter of the Houston metro area and caused $125 billion in damage, second in cost only to Hurricane Katrina (which hit New Orleans, Louisiana, in 2005). Harvey’s flooding damaged nearly 135,000 homes and 1 million cars. A Presidential Disaster Declaration (PDD) was announced for the Houston area after Harvey, which opened up access to several forms of government assistance primarily geared toward providing emergency shelter and replacing a limited amount of property.Internet Appendix Bprovides an overview of how flood insurance and Federal assistance function after disasters.

Frictions make flood insurance and Federal assistance difficult to apply to college tuition payments. First, the payouts often represent only a fraction of the damage. For example, the average FEMA individual household grant recipient received just $7,300 even though just 1 foot of flooding causes, on average, $72,162 in real and personal property damage, according to FEMA data. Moreover, the vast majority of Harvey’s flood victims lacked flood insurance, and lower-income and/or lower-credit score applicants (i.e., those at greatest risk of not being able to afford college) were often deemed ineligible for Federal disaster loans (Billings, Gallagher, and Ricketts 2022). Finally, these payouts are controlled either by the mortgage lender or by the relevant Federal agency, which earmarks the funds for specific purposes and can audit receipts.

Another question is whether student financial aid packages could be altered to reflect families’ hurricane-related losses. Harvey arrived in Houston just as the school year was beginning (August 25, 2017). Meanwhile, a student’s expected family contribution, calculated from the Free Application for Student Aid (FAFSA) to determine aid, is based on financial information from the second proceeding tax year. Thus, FAFSA forms for students enrolled as of Fall 2017 did not immediately account for the wealth shock of flooding. Not until the fall semester of 2019, would the incoming student FAFSA forms have reflected the financial condition recorded in 2017. More importantly, the FASFA does not count houses as a financial asset, so the only way FASFA forms reflect disaster losses is through drained savings (e.g., repair expenses and rent payments).

There are no Federal disaster programs that assist individuals with education expenses. Instead, students were instructed by the U.S. Department of Education to work with the financial aid office at their school to request a reassessment for financial aid. We cannot determine the extent to which colleges updated financial assistance, but anecdotal evidence is that funds for student relief were limited and, in most cases, would have amounted to less than one thousand dollars per student with demonstrated need.3More relief came 2 years after Harvey, in Q2 2019, when some Harvey-affected students received a one-time scholarship from the State of Qatar.

We study Hurricane Harvey because a large share of the flooding in Houston was unanticipated. As is documented inTable 1, only about 7% of the variation in average flooding (WAvg. flood depth) across Houston Census blocks can be explained by predetermined socioeconomic variables, credit variables, and geospatial attributes (like 100-year floodplain status, elevation, and distance to streams). Harvey flooded 30% of structures inside the floodplain and 21% of structures outside the floodplain.

Since we focus on a single natural disaster and geographic area, one may wonder how well our results generalize to other areas and natural disasters.Internet Appendix Figures A1 and A2show boxplots comparing Houston with other larger urban areas (>1 million people) that have experienced a hurricane. In particular, we construct a panel data set of all MSAs with greater than one million people in 2010 that had a major hurricane (> $1 billion in damage and at least one fatality) between 2000 and 2017. The plots indicate that Harvey is a representative large urban disaster. It exists within the interquartile range on measures, such as the predisaster college-educated share, unemployment rate, median income, as well as its post-hurricane change in the population, unemployment, and construction share of employment.

Harvey did not have a dramatic impact on employment or wages in Houston.Farrell and Greig (2018)analyze the bank accounts of Chase Bank customers and find that labor income into accounts dropped by 5% the week of Hurricane Harvey, but returned to normal within just 10 days. As additional evidence,Internet Appendix Figure A3plots employment and earnings after Harvey in Houston relative to Dallas (the largest mostly unaffected nearby city). We observe only a slight increase in employment and earnings in Houston (matching Dallas’ trend line) after Harvey, even for construction industries (Internet Appendix Figure A4). If the hurricane had substantially improved employment offers, we should see a more notable break. Narrowing the sample to college-aged workers, there is a more pronounced change in the trend for earnings after Harvey. One possible interpretation is that attending college became less attractive after Harvey because of affordability concerns, leading some young people in Houston to boost their labor supply. Regardless, our identification strategy limits the impact of this channel on the student loan analysis by comparing individuals exposed to different amounts of flooding within the same general labor market (Houston).

An important feature of Hurricane Harvey is that it did not alter the underlying economic rationale for living in Houston, allowing us to plausibly hold constant the credit and labor environment faced by flooded and nonflooded individuals. Houston experienced a net in-migration of 1.3% in the year after Hurricane Harvey (according to data from Houston.org). We find that the out-migration rate of preexisting Houston residents increases by just 0.3 percentage points in the year after Harvey, according to our credit bureau data, and it varies almost imperceptibly by flood intensity. There is also no evidence of a retraction in the supply of credit (number of new accounts) relative to the demand for credit (number of credit inquiries) after the hurricane, either overall or according to flood intensity (Internet Appendix Figure A5).
article.2. Student Debt after the Storm:
This section explores the empirical question of how a natural disaster affects student debt financing, beginning with the data and empirical strategy.

2.1 Data: Student debt

Our measures of student debt come from the Federal Reserve Bank of New York/Equifax Consumer Credit Panel (CCP), which contains information on the individual credit characteristics detailed inLee and van der Klaauw (2010). The CCP consists of Equifax credit report data for a longitudinal quarterly panel of individuals. Our data span Q4 2015 to Q2 2021. The panel is a nationally representative 5% random sample of all individuals with a Social Security number and a credit report. These credit data are joined at the Census block level with geospatial flooding, floodplain coverage, and socioeconomic data gathered from FEMA, the U.S. Geological Survey, the National Land Cover Database, and the Census, as detailed inInternet Appendix C.

The CCP contains nearly the full universe of student loans. Student debt has not (to our knowledge) been examined before in the context of a natural disaster. This is, perhaps, because student debt servicers did not consistently report data to credit bureaus until 2005, and existing literature on the household financial response to natural disasters predominately comes from Hurricane Katrina, which hit New Orleans in 2005 (Gallagher and Hartley 2017;McIntosh 2008;Deryugina, Kawano, and Levitt 2018). Student debt in our sample has a stable classification and, therefore, can be studied for the first time in the context of a natural disaster. On occasion, there is a delay (typically just one quarter) from when the loan is originated to when it is recorded in the CCP (Bricker et al. 2017). When a parent cosigns on a student loan, the loan will be assigned to both the parent and the child’s credit bureau accounts. However, the overwhelming majority of student borrowers in our sample are primary borrowers, rather than only cosigners, which is consistent with the fact that nearly all (92%) student debt is federally guaranteed (Federal “Stafford Loans”), which does not require a cosigner. Individuals who open, close, or never open student loan accounts are included in our sample with nonmissing values for student loan variables in all quarters.5

In interpreting our results, it is important to keep in mind that our results speak only to adults (18 years of age and older) with credit files as of Q2 2017. Though individuals do not need to have student debt or even a credit score to be in our sample, those with no credit history whatsoever as of Q2 2017 are not in our sample. We must make this restriction to assign individuals to a flood intensity based on their permanent residence as of the last snapshot before the hurricane (Q2 2017). To validate parallel pre-trends in our main DiD regressions, we further restrict the sample to individuals with at least 6 quarters of visible credit data before Q2 2017.

For several reasons, we believe that the degree of selection bias introduced by these restrictions is likely to be small. First, most people do not enter the CCP for the first time because of opening a student loan account. Of the 18-year-olds in the CCP as of Q2 2017, only 1.6% of them had student loans. Instead, 30% have credit cards and 6% have auto debt. A large share enter as authorized users on a parent’s credit card. Even among 19-year-olds in the CCP, the majority do not have student loans. Second, we test the effect of using an unbalanced panel, thus not requiring 6 quarters of visibility prior to Q2 2017, and found similar results (Internet Appendix Figure A6). Finally, one may be concerned that lower-income individuals are less likely to be credit visible and also may be more likely to need student loans after Harvey. However, when we look at the intensive margin treatment effect on the sample with outstanding student debt as of Q2 2017 (which should include many lower-income students), there is a relative intensive margin decline in student loan balances after flooding. If existing student loan borrowers grow their balances by less after flooding, it follows that the negative extensive margin treatment effects would also translate to individuals who are not yet credit visible as of Q2 2017. Nevertheless, our estimates should be interpreted as applying only to the credit visible at the time of the hurricane.

We must contend with an unknown degree of measurement error in assigning individuals to treatment. Most notably, since our credit data only provides geographic information at the level of a Census block, we do not observe the exact degree of flooding experienced at an individual’s address, rather we observe a proxy for their probability of having been flooded. Moreover, the exact depth and breadth of flooding merely represent FEMA’s best guess based on hydrological modeling. This type of measurement error will attenuate estimates such that our estimates should be viewed as conservative. Finally, students with a credit file address at schools located outside of the Houston metro area are omitted from our student debt sample.6These instances should be rare since credit, insurance, and even FAFSA applications generally recommend that students use their permanent (i.e., parents’) address. Thus, even if a student was not physically present in Houston for the flooding, the address in his credit file should reflect that of his parent’s house and, hence, the student’s financial exposure to flooding should be properly assigned in most cases.

In the end, we have the quarterly credit information on 129,577 individuals, of which 9,039 were college-age adults, with credit files and living in the Houston metropolitan area as of Q2 2017, just before Hurricane Harvey hit (during Q3 2017).

Table 2offers descriptive statistics for the blocks and individuals represented in our sample. The share with student loans and the average student loan balance is nearly identical across flood bins. However, the median income is about $8,547 (or one-quarter of one standard deviation) higher in the most flooded tercile (“T3”) than in nonflooded blocks (“No”). Positive selection into flooding is unexpected since wealth can help insulate households from disasters, for example, by purchasing properties at higher elevations. To the extent that wealthier households are better able to absorb the financial challenges brought by flooding, estimates based on Harvey may be conservative relative to disasters that target poorer areas. Still, despite a nontrivial degree of positive selection of wealthier areas into flooding, there is wide economic diversity within each flood bin to exploit. For example, one-quarter of the most flooded tercile of blocks had a median income of below $44,000. In our regression analyses, we use block-level controls to absorb any differences in the economies of flooded and nonflooded blocks. Moreover, our identification relies on the assumption that, in the absence of flooding, the flooded blocks would have trended similarly to nonflooded blocks. We present several pieces of evidence supporting the parallel trends assumption.

2.2 Empirical design: Student debt

Our main empirical specification is a treatment intensity difference-in-differences (DiD) of the form:

y i t = β ( T b k × P t ) + α i + D t + κ A i t 2 + ( X b × P t ) η + X b ϕ + ε i t , (1)

whereyitis a quarterly student debt outcome for individualiliving in Census blockbin quarter-yeart⁠.7Outcome variables include extensive and intensive margin measures of student debt balances.Tbis the treatment intensity,WAvg. flood depth, which is a measure of both the breadth and depth of flooding in the developed portion of a Census block (defined in more detail inInternet Appendix C). Treatment is binned as follows: a no flood group (control); the least flooded tercile of the flooded blocks (⁠Tb1⁠); the middle flooded tercile of the flooded blocks (⁠Tb2⁠); and the most flooded tercile of the flooded blocks (⁠Tb3⁠). We focus on the top tercile (⁠Tb3⁠) because of unobserved threshold effects in how flood depth relates to property damage (e.g., a car is undamaged by 6 inches of flooding but totaled by 12 inches of flooding). This is particularly important in the context of Houston, where the streets are designed as secondary storm channels (Archer 2020;Bogost 2017). As a result, in less flooded terciles, the majority of homes may have been spared damage.8Ptis the post-hurricane dummy for the 16 quarters after Q2 2017. The coefficient of interest,β⁠, captures the effect of living in a block of a particular flooding intensity relative to the outcomes of the same set of blocks during the prehurricane period and relative to the post-hurricane outcomes of blocks that did not flood.

An individual is assigned a treatment intensity according to the block where he/she had a credit file address as of Q2 2017 (the last quarterly observation before the hurricane). We, therefore, allow people to move around Houston and the rest of the country before and after the storm, holding treatment intensity constant throughout time. Individuals who did not have a permanent address in a Houston Census block at the dawn of the hurricane (Q2 2017) are excluded from the sample.

Additionally,αiis an individual fixed effect, andDtis a year-quarter fixed effect. Because an individual’s treatment intensity is time invariant, we cannot include a block-fixed effect as it would be collinear withαi⁠. We include a quadratic of age,Ait2⁠, to help absorb life cycle effects of student debt. Finally, withXb⁠, we control for several characteristics of the Census block where the individual lived as of Q2 2017 (median income, owner-occupied share, population density, minority share, median home value, and floodplain share of the developed block area). These control variables are captured pretreatment and interacted with a post-period dummy, such thatXb×Ptabsorbs any debt behaviors after the hurricane that are common to individuals from certain types of blocks regardless of flooding. Note that outside of the post-period interaction,Xbdrops out of the regression since it is collinear with the individual fixed effects. Standard errors are clustered on Census block and date.

To interpret our coefficient of interest,β⁠, as arising from a borrower-driven shift in demand for loans or repayment behavior, we must first establish that there was no shift in the supply of loans offered to flooded borrowers. This cannot be directly tested because the supply of student loans is unobserved. However, as documented inInternet Appendix Figure A5, we find no disequilibrium between demand and supply of credit in Houston after the hurricane, that is the number of new accounts opened per credit inquiry is not significantly correlated with treatment. Moreover, the majority of outstanding student loan debt (92%) is federally guaranteed (rather than private). Though capped, Federal student loans are the first line of financing, available to even very poor students, and interest rates are a fixed premium over U.S. Treasury rates as set by Congress (Amromin and Eberly 2016). These features make it unlikely that our results stem from a credit supply restriction to disaster-affected households.9

2.3 Results: Student debt

Figure 2plots DiD event study coefficients for student debt outcomes, where the sample is split into five age groups. We use age as a proxy for the likelihood of confronting a higher education investment decision. Compared to the older age groups, this first age group (⁠18≤Age<24⁠, henceforth “college-aged”) is likely to include many current and prospective undergraduate students and, hence, is of primary interest. The second and third age groups, (⁠24≤Age<30⁠) and (⁠30≤Age<35⁠), include many former students who are paying off student debt as well as a minority of current and prospective students. The older age groups are unlikely to include many current or prospective students but may include cosigners as well as some individuals who are still paying off student loans.10

Figure 2 Event study effects of flooding on student debt, by age group This figure plots event study coefficients using the individual-level balanced panel over Q4 2015–Q2 2021. Dots mark the treatment effect of top tercile flooding. Gray lines represent the 95% confidence interval around the estimate. In panel A, the dependent variable captures the extensive margin probability (in percentage terms) of having an outstanding student loan balance. In panel B, the dependent variable is student loan balances (in winsorized dollars) within the sample with nonzero student debt as of Q2 2017. An individual’s treatment intensity ( WAvg. flood depth ) is assigned according to the individual’s Census block as of the last quarter before the hurricane (Q2 2017). The coefficients for event studies can be interpreted as the effect of being in a block with top tercile flooding relative to nonflooded blocks and relative to Q2 2017. The sample for each subpanel is restricted to individuals in the age group (as of Q2 2017) noted in the subpanel title. All regressions include the full array of fixed effects and controls specified in Equation ( 1 ). Source : Federal Reserve Bank of NY/Equifax Consumer Credit Panel. Open in new tab Download slide

Panel A evaluates the extensive margin probability of having a student debt account in Houston blocks with top tercile (⁠Tb3⁠) flooding relative to blocks that did not flood and relative to Q2 2017 (the omitted period). We find that flooding is associated with a sharp relative reduction in the likelihood of having any student debt among college-aged adults. The effect begins in Q4 (the quarter after the hurricane) and grows for several quarters thereafter.11On average, over the 16 quarters post-Harvey, young adults from heavily flooded areas are around 3 percentage points less likely to have a student loan than their counterparts from the parts of Houston that did not flood. This effect size represents 7% of the mean share of the college-age sample with student debt (43%). Given that these are intent-to-treat estimates, they represent a lower bound on the effect of an individual’s property flooding. Any reluctance to hold student debt after Harvey does not reverse the next school year. Instead, those potential students whose families were likely affected by Harvey when they were ages 18–23 are still “missing loans,” relative to counterparts in nonflooded areas, 4 years later.

Panel B ofFigure 2presents event study coefficients where the dependent variable is the outstanding student debt balance (in winsorized dollars) for individuals who have nonzero outstanding student debt as of Q2 2017. If existing student borrowers pursue more debt-financed education after flooding, we would expect these coefficients to be positive. Instead, within the college-aged sample, there is a noisy relative decline in student debt balances in the most flooded tercile of blocks – implying that college-aged borrowers from heavily flooded areas grow their balances by about $866 (or 5% of the corresponding sample mean) less than their nonflooded counterparts after Harvey. These intensive margin results may reflect disenrollment, a substitution away from student loan financing, and/or paying down debt.Internet Appendix Figure A8presents the raw trends in the average student debt balances of college-aged borrowers by flood bin. Average balances are increasing within all treatment bins both before and after the hurricane. However, average balances in heavily flooded blocks increase more slowly relative to nonflooded blocks after Harvey.

From the event studies, it is clear that the impact of flooding on student debt primarily occurs on the extensive margin and within the college-aged sample. Our expectation based on the extant literature is that college-aged adults from lower-income areas are more likely to be on the margin of going to college for financial reasons and are also more likely to need student loans (Sun and Yannelis 2016;Chakrabarti et al. 2020;Denning, Marx, and Turner 2019). Therefore,Internet Appendix Figure A9digs into these extensive margin results through subsampling on neighborhood economic factors and by presenting the nonparametric means by flood bins. The graphs show that the probability of having student debt was trending upward across all flood bins before Harvey. After Harvey, it begins to flatten in the top tercile of flooded blocks. Meanwhile, in blocks with little to no flooding, the trend either flattens or continues upward. The sharpest drop in the probability of having student debt occurs in heavily flooded blocks with many owner-occupiers and a low college-educated share (Internet Appendix Figure A9).12

Table 3applies a discrete-time hazard model version of our DiD design to young adults in the credit data. Since opening a student loan may indicate a college start, we are particularly interested in whether individuals without student debt before Harvey (i.e., new prospective students) become less likely to open a student debt account when their neighborhood floods. A discrete-time hazard specification restricts the sample to individuals without student debt as of Q2 2017 and drops individuals after the first quarter when student debt appears in their credit data. According to column 1, relative to their counterparts in nonflooded areas, individuals in heavily flooded areas become 1.5 percentage points less likely to open a student debt account, on average, in the aftermath of Harvey. Through interactions, the remaining columns test for significant differential treatment effects. All secondary interactions are included or subsumed by the fixed effects. We observe a significantly more negative treatment hazard in lower-income blocks and blocks with fewer college graduates (columns 2 and 4). The treatment hazard of flooding becomes even more negative conditional on blocks with above-median owner-occupied housing (columns 3, 5, and 6).

We interpret these effects as suggestive evidence that flooding has the strongest effects on the higher education investment choices of prospective first-generation undergraduates. While we cannot observe the precise mechanism, our results are consistent with the fact that lower-income households are more likely to be on the margin of college attendance due to liquidity constraints. Lower-income households also tend to receive less in Federal disaster assistance and loans (Begley et al. 2021;Billings, Gallagher, and Ricketts 2022), which might otherwise have helped to immunize their human capital investment decisions from the financial shock of flooding.

The effect of flooding on the probability that older Houstonians hold student debt is more limited. InFigure 2, panel A, we see no clear treatment effect on individuals aged 35 or more years. However, for adults aged 24 through 34, there is a modest increase in the relative probability of having student loans in treated areas. For example, for the sample in the (⁠30≤Age<35⁠) range, a 0.75 percentage point increase in the probability of having student loans represents 2.5% of the mean rate of student loan debt (30%). On the intensive margin, in panel B ofFigure 2, there is also small and fleeting relative increase in student debt dollar balances among non-college-aged borrowers. To better understand this result,Internet Appendix Figure A10plots the nonparametric mean probability of student debt by flood bin for the (⁠30≤Age<35⁠) sample. We find a positive relative effect only among those borrowers with preexisting student debt and, particularly, in high owner-occupied areas. One interpretation is that the flood triggered a slower pay-down (and more accrued interest) on old balances. This interpretation is consistent with the forbearance offered on Federal student loans for up to 1 year after Harvey.
article.3. Higher Education Attainment after the:
In this section, we test for changes in enrollment and completion at Texas public universities and colleges after Hurricane Harvey. We also explore variation in enrollment effects according to the earnings profiles of different majors.

3.1 Data and empirical strategy: Higher education

Data come from the Texas Higher Education Coordinating Board (THECB) and includes all 110 Texas “public universities” and “community colleges,” as defined by the THECB, reporting data during fall semester 2014 through spring semester 2021.13The panel data set captures school-level outcomes over time. Key variables include enrollment and graduation rates.

Importantly, the THECB data include the geographic origin of the school’s enrollees, allowing us to differentiate schools according to their student body’s exposure to Hurricane Harvey (treatment).14For each school, we aggregate enrollment from the 40 Texas counties that were most damaged by Hurricane Harvey.15Dividing a school’s disaster county enrollment by its total enrollment as of the spring semester of 2017 (the last “academic year” semester before the hurricane) gives a sense of the school’s pretreatment enrollment exposure to the disaster.Internet Appendix Table A1presents descriptive statistics for this data set. Universities and colleges each had, on average, 26%–27% of their enrollment coming from disaster-affected counties as of spring semester 2017. About half of schools with above-median enrollment exposure to the disaster, including universities, like the University of Texas at Austin, and colleges, like Midland College, are not, themselves, located in the 40 disaster-affected counties yet draw a large share of their student body from high schools in the affected counties. We verify that more and less treated schools exhibit similar pre-trends on all outcomes before Hurricane Harvey.

We apply a treatment intensity difference-in-differences model of the form:

y s , t = β ( T s × P t ) + α s + D t + λ ( U N I V s × D t ) + ϕ ( R E G I O N r × D t ) + ε s , t , (2)

whereys,tis the outcome variable for each school,s⁠, on each date,t⁠.16Since the hurricane struck in August of 2017, the post-period begins in the Fall semester of 2017. InInternet Appendix D, we present a variation on this specification that allows us to study enrollment in each of the majors offered at each school (i.e., where the outcome variable, enrollment, is captured at the school-major-time level,ys,m,t⁠).

Treatment intensity,Ts⁠, is a dummy for having an above-median disaster-county-enrollment share across schools within the same type (universities or colleges). We compare schools within school type to ensure that our results are robust to differential variance by school type (universities or colleges).17To provide a sense of the distribution of treatment effects, we also present results where treatment is a continuous percentile rank on the unit interval. Interacting treatment,Ts⁠, with a post-hurricane dummy,Pt⁠, provides the post-hurricane differential average effect of having a larger share of students originating from disaster-affected counties.

Regressions include a school fixed effect,αs⁠, and a time dummy (either semester or year, depending on the highest frequency available for the outcome variable),Dt⁠. We include interactions of school type and time,UNIVs×Dt⁠, to absorb any changes in average outcomes at particular school types (i.e., universities vs. colleges). Finally, we absorb THECB’s geographical education regions interacted with time,REGIONr×Dt⁠, to control for regional trends and the fact that schools in particular regions (e.g., the “gulf coast” region) were more likely to have been in proximity to flooding.

We test the robustness of our main results to dropping from the sample those 29 schools that have campuses located within the 40 counties that severely flooded. This ensures that our findings are primarily attributable to student demand for human capital rather than to supply-side factors (e.g., schools being closed because of flooding). In addition, since treatment intensity is correlated with a school’s proximity to the gulf coast, our preferred specification uses standard errors that are clustered on Texas higher education regions in each period (forming 140 clusters), which helps absorb any correlation in the residuals caused by schools within the same region being subject to common economic shocks that may influence enrollment.

In interpreting these results, it is important to bear in mind that we cannot directly observe the differential human capital obtained by flooded versus nonflooded individual Texas students. Hence, this school-level specification leaves open the potential for common shocks associated with a school’s treatment intensity that might independently affect outcome variables. For example, if the alumni at more treated schools stop donating after a disaster, the enrollment at those same schools might be negatively affected. We cannot rule out this kind of indirect channel. Also, since our data are insufficiently granular to observe the major choices of individual students over time, we cannot say whether our school-major-time level results stem from major shifts among previously enrolled students or compositional changes in the types of students who enroll.18

3.2 Results: Higher education

If coming from a disaster-affected county causes students to forgo or delay education, we should see a post-hurricane decline in enrollment and completion at schools with a greater share of students from disaster counties. InFigure 3treatment is a binary indicator that the school’s disaster-county-enrollment share is above the median. Results in panels A and B indicate a negative relationship between treatment and enrollment after Harvey. Consistent with the student debt results, the relative drop in enrollment is being driven by college-aged students. The treatment effect on enrollment begins immediately, in the fall of 2017, and does not rebound. Enrollment in the most treated schools remains relatively low 3 years after the hurricane and then drops further in the fall of 2020 and the spring of 2021, which is the end of our sample period. Thus, it appears that the pandemic only exacerbated trends sparked by Harvey.19

Less enrollment could be attributable both to fewer college starts and to dropouts. This latter effect, if present, should be reflected in a lower graduation rate. The event study inFigure 3, panel C, shows a relative decline in the graduation rate among more treated schools after Harvey. Though these estimates may appear only marginal in significance, both the enrollment and graduation rate effects are larger in magnitude and statistically stronger when treatment is measured as a continuous percentile rank instead of as a binary above-median indicator (seeInternet Appendix Figure A11). One possible explanation for the drop in graduation rates after Harvey is that it may take students longer to complete their degrees. Panel D, however, indicates that disaster exposure is associated with little change in the average number of years taken by the school’s students to complete their degrees.

Finally, panels E and F suggest no change in the average student loan balance of enrolled students or the share of graduates with debt at more disaster-enrollment-exposed schools after Harvey. If, after flooding, students can reduce their use of student debt through means other than enrollment – for example, by using disaster loans, insurance payouts, or financial aid – we should observe a drop in the student debt balancesofenrolledstudentsand/or a smaller share of graduates with debt at more treated schools. We do not. These results are evidence that the relative decline in the extensive margin of student loans, documented in Section2.3, is more likely stemming from a disenrollment channel rather than a financing substitution channel.

Figure 4presents the raw trends in average enrollment by treatment intensity. Despite strong seasonality, the leftmost panel depicts an overall upward trend in average school-level enrollment. The plotted data in the middle and rightmost panels are residuals from a regression of enrollment on semester-year dummies interacted with school type. Consistent with the DiD results, the raw trends show that schools with more enrollment exposure to disaster counties grow their enrollment by less after Harvey.Internet Appendix Figure A12shows that these raw trends are similar for aggregate enrollment (instead of average enrollment) as well as for graduation rates. Finally, if our results are related to disaster exposure and not to, for example, large urban-area education trends, we should find no post-Harvey effect from enrollment exposure to the Dallas area. Event studies inInternet Appendix Figure A13document that a school’s enrollment share from Dallas-area counties as of Spring 2017 (placebo treatment) has no significant effect on its post-Harvey enrollment or completion.

To help interpret the magnitudes of the DiD estimates,Table 4presents the associated post-period average treatment effects. After Harvey, schools with an above-median enrollment exposure to the disaster grow their enrollment by 4.8% less relative to other schools (column 1). For perspective, this effect size is similar to the aggregate 4% enrollment loss at public universities and colleges recorded during Fall 2020 of the COVID-19 pandemic, which is widely recognized as one of the most important national shocks to higher education enrollment in recent times.20Column 2 shows that the enrollment effect after Harvey is owed to the college-aged cohort – the same cohort that experiences a relative decline in their use of student debt (see Section2.3). We further explore this result inInternet Appendix Table A2, where we test for treatment effects on enrollment by undergraduate status (first-time, transfer, and continuing). Unfortunately, this detail is only available for some public universities and only during fall semesters. Nevertheless, similar to effects observed during the COVID-19 pandemic, we find that the majority (63%=372/587) of the post-Harvey drop in average undergraduate enrollment at more treated public universities is attributable to first-time undergraduates.

We run a series of robustness checks. Column 3 documents that our choice of clustering generates more conservative standard errors (resulting in smallert-statistics) than a heteroskedasticity-consistent (robust) alternative when the outcome variable is enrollment. Column 4 shows that enrollment effects remain statistically significant, though smaller in magnitude (5.6% vs. 9.8%), when we exclude the 27% of Texas public universities and colleges that are located in disaster-affected counties (dropping over a quarter of the sample, including many of the most treated schools). It is, therefore, unlikely that the physical toll of the disaster on school buildings and faculty homes is the primary factor driving the overall treatment effect on enrollment. Moreover, the fact that we find large and significant treatment effects even within the pool of Texas schools that are located outside of disaster counties, implies that students are not merely shifting their enrollment to schools outside of the physical range of the disaster.21

According to columns 5–7, the graduation rate is 1.8 percentage points lower at more treated schools after Harvey. At the same time, estimates in columns 8–10 indicate that students in more treated schools take only about 0.09 additional years, which is tiny relative to a mean of 4.37 years, to complete their degrees. Together, these results suggest that students at more disaster-county-enrollment-exposed schools are less likely to complete.

Table 5explores price sensitivity using variation in the treatment effect on school enrollment by school type and tuition rank. Column 1 indicates that the negative impact of disaster-county exposure on enrollment is no worse for community colleges relative to universities. In fact, according to column 2, the negative treatment effect on the enrollment of students aged 18-21 is milder for community colleges. As we will explain in Section4, this result conflicts with the predicted effect of a labor market opportunity costs mechanism. Instead, our findings align with a liquidity constraints mechanism since community colleges are a lower-cost option for constrained students.22

3.3 Results: Major choice

Finally, we test for enrollment shifts based on the earnings profile of different majors. Specifically, we estimate the post-Harvey treatment effect on enrollment in each school-major combination, where majors are divided at the median according to expected post-graduate earnings – captured 1 year, 5 years, and 10 years after graduation.Internet Appendix Dprovides more detail on the empirical strategy at this more granular school-major-time level and presents the associated DiD regression estimates.

Briefly, the results inInternet Appendix Dindicate that low-earnings majors lose students and high-earnings majors gain students when the school is more treated. For example, the average low-earnings major loses 23 students, while the average high-earnings major gains 15 students at more treated schools relative to less treated schools. A net loss of eight students equates to 4.3% of the mean, such that the school-major-time level effect closely resembles the more aggregated school-time level effect (4.8%) presented inTable 4. Interestingly, the drop in enrollment in low-earnings majors is driven by community colleges. Universities, in contrast, see relatively fewer undeclared students, consistent with evidence that treatment leads to relatively fewer first-time undergraduates (Internet Appendix Table A2). Importantly,Internet Appendix Ddocuments a stronger reaction to longer-term earnings measures than to starting salaries. Therefore, we present results based on a 10-year post-graduate earnings split.

Figure 5summarizes the key findings, plotting the average enrollment in the low 10-year earnings college majors. Though average enrollment in these majors is increasing until the start of the pandemic (leftmost panel), average enrollment in low-earnings majors begins to flatten at more treated colleges after Harvey (middle panel). The rightmost panel depicts an immediate decline in average enrollment in these majors, relative to the residualized trend, after Harvey.

Rather than attempt to list several hundred majors in our THECB data in one chart, we use broad categories of majors, ordered according to 10-year median earnings for the median major within that Classification of Instructional Programs (CIP) group. Internet Appendix Figure A14digs deeper into these findings by plotting individual majors, instead of categories of majors (CIPs rather than CIP groups), that experienced particularly large enrollment changes. Panel A shows that sign language, general studies, humanities, and liberal arts majors each become a much smaller share of average enrollment at high treatment schools relative to low treatment schools after the disaster. Meanwhile, biosciences, business administration, prenursing, and engineering each increased their relative enrollment after Harvey. Prenursing, for example, becomes a 3 percentage point larger share of enrollment, on average, at more treated schools relative to less treated schools. Panel B looks at raw changes in enrollment numbers and drops the second difference, restricting the sample to only high treatment schools. We see the same diagonal pattern – signaling that the major shifting in high treatment schools occurs both in absolute and relative terms.

In summary, these results are consistent with a natural disaster causing a reduction in overall human capital investment as well as a reoptimization of remaining investments, in turn, leaving less demand for student loans. These findings may also have implications for income inequality. Using the Kolmogorov-Smirnov tests of the equality of distributions, we find a statistically significant(p-value <.001) rightward shift in the cumulative distribution function of implied 10-year earnings for enrolled students at more treated schools. Across the distribution, students enrolled in high treatment schools have higher implied earnings after the hurricane relative to before the hurricane and relative to less treated schools. When coupled with the finding of a relative drop in enrollment at more treated schools, these findings could foreshadow a widening earnings gap between college and noncollege graduates.
article.4. Discussion of Mechanisms:
The extant literature offers a conceptual framework that helps parse the microeconomic mechanisms behind our results. First, we address the possibility that our findings can be explained by changes in the trade-offs between work and study.Charles, Hurst, and Notowidigdo (2018)show theoretically that, all else equal, an improvement in labor market opportunities for young adults should primarily affect students on the margin of going to college at all (i.e., community college students) and have little effect on investment in the type of bachelor’s degree training that is more commonly attained from universities.25Consistent with their model,Charles, Hurst, and Notowidigdo (2018)find that expansions in labor market opportunities coincident with housing booms reduce college enrollment, with effects concentrated at community colleges. In contrast, after Harvey, we do not find that treated community colleges lost proportionally more enrollment than treated universities after Harvey, as would be expected if Harvey had dramatically altered the work-study trade-off.26

A more likely mechanism involves liquidity constraints. Using hedonic regressions on housing transactions in Houston, we estimate a discount of at least 12% in early 2018 on properties with 1 foot or more of flooding (Internet Appendix E). As flooding wipes out home equity, it eliminates a key source of liquidity for homeowners seeking to finance education. A large body of recent work has tested the importance of liquidity constraints on college attainment with mixed results. Most relevant to our study,Lovenheim (2011)andLovenheim and Reynolds (2013)document the increased college attendance of students from low-income families experiencing a boom in housing wealth (see alsoLochner and Monge-Naranjo 2011;Sun and Yannelis 2016;Manoli and Turner 2018;Chakrabarti et al. 2020). On the other hand,Hilger (2016)finds that parental layoffs have only small effects on college-going (see alsoStinebrickner and Stinebrickner 2008;Cameron and Taber 2004;Bulman et al. 2021).

However, in the setting of a natural disaster, liquidity constraints may be more likely to bind than after layoffs or cyclical housing busts since being unable to live in one’s home after a disaster entails a dramatic increase in living and repair expenses (del Valle, Scharlemann, and Shore 2019;Collier and Ellis 2021). Recessions, by comparison, are characterized by a drop in spending (Mian, Rao, and Sufi 2013;Aladangady 2017). Thus, to the extent that liquidity constraints bind for some flooded families, we should expect to find less college attainment after Harvey. We should also observe a shift in enrollment away from the most expensive schools (i.e., high-priced universities). Our findings are consistent with these predictions.

Financial shocks – in the form of housing busts, tuition increases, and changes in interest rates – have been shown to alter the financing of education (Bhutta and Keys 2016;Amromin, Eberly, and Mondragon 2017;Chakrabarti et al. 2020). Most notably,Amromin, Eberly, and Mondragon (2017)document that, during the Great Recession, educational investments were financed through additional student debt. They chronicle a 50% increase in new student loans between 2007 and 2010, which they attribute, in part, to a loss of home equity-based financing.27Hence, the destruction of home equity by Harvey would have, presumably, pushed some households to substitute into student loans (particularly since student loan payments can be deferred until after graduation). However, a financing substitution channel will only be detected if Harvey-affected students remain enrolled at schools with similar costs. If liquidity constraints bind, causing more marginal students to disenroll or move to cheaper schools, the net effect may be a reduction in the use of (need for) student debt in more flooded neighborhoods relative to neighborhoods that escaped flooding. Our results are consistent with this latter explanation.

Finally, a negative shock to house values destroys not only liquidity (collateral value) but also wealth, and wealth effects could separately affect education choices. A large literature supports the view that households derive consumption value from college, with the consumption component of education being larger for wealthier households (Arcidiacono 2004;Jacob and Lefgren 2007;Alstadsaeter 2011;Gong et al. 2019;Bulman et al. 2021). Hence, as households lose wealth to flooding, we should expect more of their spending on higher education to be aimed at investment. Such an intensive margin adjustment is consistent with our finding of a shift in enrollment toward majors that lead to higher-paying jobs. Similarly, a change in expectations about the risk of losing future wealth to flooding (Gallagher, 2014) might lead students to try to insulate themselves financially through their choice of major. Indeed, similar movements in major choice have been observed in the context of recessions (Bedard and Herman 2008;Liu, Sun, and Winters 2019;Ersoy 2020;Blom, Cadena, and Keys 2021), which share these same features of wealth loss and potential shifts in expectations.28
article.5. Conclusion:
This paper spotlights a novel connection between climate change and human capital investment using geographic variation in exposure to flooding from Hurricane Harvey. Our results point to a decrease in the quantity but an increase in the intensity of investments in human capital after Harvey.

Specifically, we find that the use of student debt by college-aged (under age 24) adults declines by 3 percentage points (or about 7%) in the heavily flooded parts of Houston relative to the nonflooded parts. After flooding, the “missing loans” are most prevalent in those owner-occupied parts of Houston where median incomes are low and fewer college graduates reside, indicating greater impacts on first-generation prospective students. We offer evidence that the leverage reductions after flooding can be explained, at least in part, by a decline in the quantity of higher education attained. There is a relative decline in enrollment and completion at schools that, before Harvey, drew more students from disaster-affected counties. And, consistent with our student debt results, the treatment effects on enrollment are more negative within the college-aged cohort and at more expensive universities. These results point to an important role for liquidity constraints in governing higher education investment choices after a natural disaster.

Fewer college graduates might negatively affect economic growth, exacerbate inequality, and lead to a “brain drain” in disaster-prone areas. At the same time, however, we show that this natural disaster shifted enrollment toward higher-earnings majors, such that there was an upward adjustment in the intensity of the average educational investment made after Harvey. One interpretation is that the wealth shock from flooding lead to less education “consumption.” It is, therefore, not yet clear that longer-term aggregate productivity and earnings are negatively affected by human capital decisions made in the wake of disasters. Estimating the long-term distributional consequences and the net welfare effects of disasters are important avenues for future research.
